Spinning Shared Memory Synchronization (tmc/spin.h)
[Tilera Multicore Components (TMC&tm;) Library]

Detailed Description

Spin-based memory synchronization primitives for use with one-task-per-core applications.

TMC provides both "kernel scheduler" and "spinning" implementations of shared memory synchronization primitives. The spinning variants, described in this section, do not yield the core (that is, context-switch to another process or thread) if a resource is unavailable. Instead, they will poll continually until forward progress can be made. Spinning primitives can be more efficient than the versions that interact with the kernel scheduler, but they are less flexible.

The spinning synchronization primitives should only be used by tasks that do not share a core with any other tasks. Failure to observe this rule can result in severe performance problems when, for example, a task holding a tmc_spin_mutex_t is context-swapped out for a tenth of a second so another task can run. In such cases, no other task can access the mutex until the holding task is swapped back in. This problem could potentially bring an entire application to a complete stop for many milliseconds. The kernel scheduling synchronization primitives avoid this problem by informing the kernel scheduler that particular tasks are blocked on the mutex, causing them to yield the core so the holding task can swap back in and release the mutex.

The spin-based synchronization primitives work best when running in Tilera's Zero-Overhead Linux™ (ZOL) environment. When running on ZOL tiles, it is possible to avoid the latency "hiccups" that might result from a Linux timer tick firing while a task is holding a spin mutex.

To synchronize between a ZOL tile and a regular tile, you may wish to use a tmc_sync_mutex_t lock and use the tmc_sync_mutex_spin_lock() API on the ZOL tiles.

Mutex Performance Recommendations

For some applications, the choice of mutex implementation can have a significant performance impact. In general, the spin-based mutex implementations are faster than the tmc_sync_mutex_t or pthread_mutex_t implementations, but they can only be used on ZOL tiles.

<tmc/spin.h> provides two mutex implementations: tmc_spin_mutex_t and tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t. The queued mutex requires slightly more overhead but provides much better fairness. Fairness can yield more predictable performance in applications where a lock is highly contended, since no one core tends to win the mutex more than the others. Both mutex implementations perform better when lock contention is low.

In general, we recommend that applications use tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t rather than tmc_spin_mutex_t. tmc_spin_mutex_t should only be used in cases where mutex overhead is truly critical and lock contention is low.

For applications that require read-write locks, the tmc_spin_rwlock_t type is also provided. It allows multiple read-lockers simultaneously, but only a single write-locker, which then also excludes any read-lockers. The performance of lock acquisition/release for read locks is generally slightly slower than the spinning mutexes described above when unloaded, and intermediate between pure spin and queued spin mutexes when contended.

The following table provides a comparison of mutex performance on the TILE-Gx processor. For each type of mutex, we measure the number of cycles required to acquire a lock, bump a counter, and release the lock. Measurements are provided for several situations, including: a single core locking a mutex homed on that core, a single core locking a mutex homed on a remote core, and many cores (~50) all contending for the same lock. Read-locks of tmc_spin_rwlock_t are included, though they are not "mutexes" properly speaking.

It is frequently the case that atomic instructions can provide even faster updates to shared data structures than mutexes. Please see the <arch/atomic.h> header documentation for more information on the available atomic operations.

Note: the performance of many cores contending shows the aggregate throughput of lock acquire/release for the single lock, thus for example a value of 200 would mean that on average, some core is acquiring and releasing the lock every 200 cycles.

Mutex Type Single Core Local Single Core Remote Many Core Contended Fair
tmc_spin_mutex_t 44 155 200 No
tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t 48 160 300 Yes
tmc_spin_rwlock_t (read) 44 160 65 N/A
tmc_sync_mutex_t 50 165 250 No
pthread_mutex_t 191 345 500 No

Data Structures

struct  tmc_spin_mutex_t
 A shared memory spinlock data structure. More...
struct  tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t
 A shared memory, spinning, fair mutex. More...
struct  tmc_spin_rwlock_t
 A shared memory reader/writer spinlock data structure. More...
struct  tmc_spin_barrier_t
 A shared memory, spin-based barrier structure. More...
struct  tmc_spin_cond_t
 A shared memory, spin-based condition variable. More...

Defines

#define TMC_SPIN_MUTEX_INIT   { 0 }
 Static initializer for a tmc_spin_mutex_t.
#define TMC_SPIN_QUEUED_MUTEX_INIT   { 0 }
 Static initializer for a tmc_spin_mutex_t.
#define TMC_SPIN_RWLOCK_INIT   { 0 }
 Static initializer for a tmc_spin_rwlock_t.
#define TMC_SPIN_BARRIER_INIT(NUM_TASKS)   { __TMC_SPIN_BARRIER_VALUE(0, (NUM_TASKS), (NUM_TASKS)) }
 Static initializer for a tmc_spin_barrier_t.
#define TMC_SPIN_COND_INIT   { { 0 }, { 0 } }
 Static initializer for a tmc_spin_cond_t.

Functions

void tmc_spin_mutex_init (tmc_spin_mutex_t *mutex)
 Initialize a mutex to the unlocked state.
void tmc_spin_mutex_lock (tmc_spin_mutex_t *mutex)
 Lock a mutex.
int tmc_spin_mutex_trylock (tmc_spin_mutex_t *mutex)
 Try to lock a mutex, but return immediately.
void tmc_spin_mutex_unlock (tmc_spin_mutex_t *mutex)
 Release an already held mutex.
void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_init (tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *mutex)
 Initialize a mutex to the unlocked state.
void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_lock (tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *mutex)
 Lock a queued mutex.
int tmc_spin_queued_mutex_trylock (tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *mutex)
 Try to lock a queued mutex, but return immediately.
void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_unlock (tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *mutex)
 Release an already held queued mutex.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_init (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Initialize an rwlock to the unlocked state.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_rdlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Lock an rwlock for reading.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_wrlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Lock an rwlock for writing.
int tmc_spin_rwlock_tryrdlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Try to read-lock an rwlock, but return immediately.
int tmc_spin_rwlock_trywrlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Try to write-lock an rwlock, but return immediately.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_rdunlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Release an rwlock that was locked for reading.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_wrunlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Release an rwlock that was locked for writing.
void tmc_spin_rwlock_unlock (tmc_spin_rwlock_t *rwlock)
 Release an rwlock that was locked for reading or writing.
void tmc_spin_barrier_init (tmc_spin_barrier_t *barrier, unsigned num_tasks)
 Initialize a barrier to the state in which no tasks have arrived at the barrier.
int tmc_spin_barrier_wait (tmc_spin_barrier_t *barrier)
 Wait until the specified number of tasks have arrived at a barrier.
void tmc_spin_cond_init (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond)
 Initialize a condvar to have no waiters.
int tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_spin (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond, tmc_spin_mutex_t *lock, const struct timespec *abstime)
 Wait until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.
int tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_queued (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond, tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *lock, const struct timespec *abstime)
 Wait until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.
int tmc_spin_cond_wait_spin (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond, tmc_spin_mutex_t *lock)
 Wait forever until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.
int tmc_spin_cond_wait_queued (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond, tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *lock)
 Wait forever until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.
int tmc_spin_cond_signal (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond)
 Signal one of the tasks that are blocked on the condition variable.
int tmc_spin_cond_broadcast (tmc_spin_cond_t *cond)
 Signal all of the tasks that are blocked on the condition variable.

Define Documentation

#define TMC_SPIN_MUTEX_INIT   { 0 }

Static initializer for a tmc_spin_mutex_t.

#define TMC_SPIN_QUEUED_MUTEX_INIT   { 0 }

Static initializer for a tmc_spin_mutex_t.

#define TMC_SPIN_RWLOCK_INIT   { 0 }

Static initializer for a tmc_spin_rwlock_t.

#define TMC_SPIN_BARRIER_INIT ( NUM_TASKS   )     { __TMC_SPIN_BARRIER_VALUE(0, (NUM_TASKS), (NUM_TASKS)) }

Static initializer for a tmc_spin_barrier_t.

Parameters:
NUM_TASKS The number of processes or threads that must call tmc_spin_barrier_wait() on this barrier in order for the barrier operation to complete.
Examples:
tmc/queue/bench_one_to_one.c.

#define TMC_SPIN_COND_INIT   { { 0 }, { 0 } }

Static initializer for a tmc_spin_cond_t.

Function Documentation

void tmc_spin_mutex_init ( tmc_spin_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Initialize a mutex to the unlocked state.

Parameters:
mutex The shared-memory mutex object to be initialized.

void tmc_spin_mutex_lock ( tmc_spin_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Lock a mutex.

This function polls repeatedly until the lock is available.

A task will self-deadlock if it attempts to re-lock a locked mutex.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.

int tmc_spin_mutex_trylock ( tmc_spin_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Try to lock a mutex, but return immediately.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.
Returns:
0 on success, non-zero if the lock could not be obtained.

void tmc_spin_mutex_unlock ( tmc_spin_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Release an already held mutex.

Any task (not just the mutex owner) can unlock a locked mutex.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.

void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_init ( tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Initialize a mutex to the unlocked state.

Parameters:
mutex The shared-memory mutex object to be initialized.

void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_lock ( tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Lock a queued mutex.

This function polls repeatedly until the lock is available. If the lock is unavailable, the calling task joins a list of waiting tasks and spins until its turn arrives. This scheme achieves better fairness than a simple spinlock.

A task will self-deadlock if it attempts to re-lock a locked mutex.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.

int tmc_spin_queued_mutex_trylock ( tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Try to lock a queued mutex, but return immediately.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.
Returns:
0 on success, non-zero if the lock could not be obtained.

void tmc_spin_queued_mutex_unlock ( tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  mutex  ) 

Release an already held queued mutex.

Any task (not just the mutex owner) can unlock a locked mutex.

Parameters:
mutex The mutex object.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_init ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Initialize an rwlock to the unlocked state.

Parameters:
rwlock The shared-memory rwlock object to be initialized.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_rdlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Lock an rwlock for reading.

This function polls repeatedly until the lock is available for read, potentially sharing the lock with other tasks that also have it locked for reading.

A task can recursively re-acquire a read lock on a read-locked rwlock. In principle, the reader count can overflow, but only when acquiring 2^31 reader locks on a given rwlock.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_wrlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Lock an rwlock for writing.

This function polls repeatedly until the lock is available for write. Once a task calls this function, no other reader can acquire the lock. The lock will block until all the readers release their locks, then it will be acquired as an exclusive write lock for the caller.

A task will self-deadlock if it attempts to re-lock a write-locked rwlock.

Note that we do not enforce fairness on the would-be writers when acquiring the lock; instead, they all just spin trying to acquire it.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.

int tmc_spin_rwlock_tryrdlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Try to read-lock an rwlock, but return immediately.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.
Returns:
0 on success, non-zero if the lock could not be obtained.

int tmc_spin_rwlock_trywrlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Try to write-lock an rwlock, but return immediately.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.
Returns:
0 on success, non-zero if the lock could not be obtained.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_rdunlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Release an rwlock that was locked for reading.

Any task (not just the rwlock owner) can unlock a locked rwlock.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_wrunlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Release an rwlock that was locked for writing.

Any task (not just the rwlock owner) can unlock a locked rwlock.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.

void tmc_spin_rwlock_unlock ( tmc_spin_rwlock_t *  rwlock  ) 

Release an rwlock that was locked for reading or writing.

Note that this routine is somewhat slower than the routines that are specific to unlocking read- or write-locks.

Any task (not just the rwlock owner) can unlock a locked rwlock.

Parameters:
rwlock The rwlock object.

void tmc_spin_barrier_init ( tmc_spin_barrier_t *  barrier,
unsigned  num_tasks 
)

Initialize a barrier to the state in which no tasks have arrived at the barrier.

It is almost always a mistake to re-initialize a barrier, unless the application can guarantee that no other thread is currently waiting at the barrier. Since barriers are reusable, it is rarely helpful to reinitialize a particular barrier.

Parameters:
barrier A shared-memory barrier object to be initialized.
num_tasks The number of processes or threads that must call tmc_spin_barrier_wait() on this barrier in order for the barrier operation to complete.
Examples:
mpipe/forward/app.c, mpipe/forward/app_gx8072.c, and trio/gxpci_mmi/mmi.c.

int tmc_spin_barrier_wait ( tmc_spin_barrier_t *  barrier  ) 

Wait until the specified number of tasks have arrived at a barrier.

This call may be used multiple times for a single barrier. Each time all the tasks will have to arrive at the barrier again before any task can proceed.

Parameters:
barrier The barrier object.
Returns:
1 in the first process to exit the barrier, 0 in all other processes.
Examples:
mpipe/forward/app.c, mpipe/forward/app_gx8072.c, tmc/queue/bench_one_to_one.c, and trio/gxpci_mmi/mmi.c.

void tmc_spin_cond_init ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond  ) 

Initialize a condvar to have no waiters.

Parameters:
cond The condition variable to be initialized.

int tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_spin ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond,
tmc_spin_mutex_t *  lock,
const struct timespec *  abstime 
)

Wait until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.

Must be called with the given tmc_spin_mutex_t lock held. The lock is released while the routine is spinning waiting to to be signalled, and then reacquired before the routine returns.

The same lock must be used for concurrent tmc_spin_cond_wait_spin() or tmc_spin_cond_wait_queued() operations on the same condition variable.

Parameters:
cond The condition variable being waited on.
lock The locked mutex used to guard the conditional variable.
abstime Absolute time at which to return with a timeout, or NULL to wait forever.
Returns:
0 on success, ETIMEDOUT if the timeout expires, or EOVERFLOW if there are too many waiters.

int tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_queued ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond,
tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  lock,
const struct timespec *  abstime 
)

Wait until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.

See tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_spin() for API details.

int tmc_spin_cond_wait_spin ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond,
tmc_spin_mutex_t *  lock 
)

Wait forever until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.

See tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_spin() for API details.

int tmc_spin_cond_wait_queued ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond,
tmc_spin_queued_mutex_t *  lock 
)

Wait forever until the given tmc_spin_cond_t has been signaled.

See tmc_spin_cond_timedwait_spin() for API details.

int tmc_spin_cond_signal ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond  ) 

Signal one of the tasks that are blocked on the condition variable.

Normally the same lock should be held when calling this function as is held by the tmc_spin_cond_wait_spin() or tmc_spin_cond_wait_queued() callers. If not, it's possible that a concurrent call to this method and one of the wait methods would result in a waiter left waiting.

Parameters:
cond The condition variable being waited on
Returns:
0 on success

int tmc_spin_cond_broadcast ( tmc_spin_cond_t *  cond  ) 

Signal all of the tasks that are blocked on the condition variable.

Normally the same lock should be held when calling this function as is held by the tmc_spin_cond_wait_spin() or tmc_spin_cond_wait_queued() callers. If not, it's possible that a concurrent call to this method and one of the wait methods would result in a waiter left waiting.

Parameters:
cond The condition variable being waited on
Returns:
0 on success
